ukhab_identity.secondary_codes_notes: 10 = scattered scrub (where present at margins); 14 = bracken (where present as encroachment — negative indicator)

sources.title: British Plant Communities Vol. 2: Mires and Heaths
sources.specific_claim: NVC community codes H8-H22 definitions and species compositions for upland heath

ecology.description: Upland heathland is a semi-natural habitat dominated by dwarf shrubs, principally heather (Calluna vulgaris), occurring above the enclosed improved farmland but below the blanket bog zone, typically on free-draining, acidic, nutrient-poor soils between 200m and 700m altitude. It is a plagioclimax community shaped by millennia of grazing, burning, and cutting. Structurally it comprises a mosaic of heather at different growth stages (pioneer, building, mature, degenerate) interspersed with grasses, sedges, mosses, and lichens. In the wider upland landscape it forms a critical ecological link between enclosed improved grassland below and blanket bog or montane heath above.
condition_indicators_good: Calluna vulgaris dominant with diverse age structure — pioneer, building, mature, and degenerate phases all present
Erica tetralix (cross-leaved heath) present in wetter patches
Empetrum nigrum (crowberry) present, especially at higher altitude
Sphagnum spp. present in hollows and wetter areas
Lichens (Cladonia spp., Cetraria islandica) present on drier ground and older heather
Molinia caerulea and Nardus stricta low cover — <10% combined where heather is target dominant
Evidence of ground-nesting birds (red grouse, golden plover, curlew, dunlin at higher altitude)
Healthy invertebrate populations — particularly bees, day-flying moths, bog bush cricket
Open, well-structured sward with bare ground patches for reptiles and invertebrates
condition_indicators_poor: Heather all of one age class, particularly all degenerate (leggy, collapsing, moribund)
High Molinia caerulea (purple moor-grass) cover >25% — outcompeting and suppressing heather regeneration
Nardus stricta (mat grass) dominant patches replacing heather
Poaching, compaction, and bare peat from overgrazing or inappropriately timed cattle
Rush (Juncus) encroachment indicating waterlogging or improved drainage issues
Absence of Sphagnum in areas that should be moist enough to support it
Absence of bilberry — indicator of nutrient enrichment or intensive burning history
Rank continuous heather with no structural diversity
Heavy grazing pressure creating a grassy heath with very low heather cover
ecosystem_functions: Carbon storage — deep peat soils beneath heather accumulate long-term carbon. Intact heathland is a significant carbon store; degraded heathland is a source.
Water regulation — heather canopy intercepts rainfall, slows surface runoff, reduces flood peaks downstream
Water quality — low-nutrient heathland catchments produce exceptionally clean, soft water
Habitat for specialist wildlife — red grouse, golden plover, merlin, short-eared owl, hen harrier, curlew, mountain hare, adder, common lizard
Pollinator habitat — heather is a major late-summer nectar source critical for bees and many upland moth species
Invertebrate diversity — supports specialist communities not found in improved grassland
Cultural and landscape value — integral to the character of the Scottish and English uplands, supporting traditional land management practices
ecology.whole_system_role: In integrated landscape design, upland heathland occupies the mid-altitude transitional zone between enclosed productive grassland and high-altitude blanket bog or montane heath. It provides structural and functional diversity in the landscape mosaic. Well-managed heathland at appropriate stocking density creates diverse sward structure — a mix of heights and openness — that supports specialist wildlife unavailable in more productive habitats. It buffers hydrological flows from higher ground, contributes to catchment water quality, and provides winter forage for hardy grazing animals when enclosed grassland is rested. In regenerative grazing plans it often functions as the low-input, high-ecological-value component of a multi-habitat system, balanced against more productive swards lower in the landscape.

successional_neighbours.note: Transitions to mountain heaths and willow scrub at higher altitude or on more exposed ground
Grades into blanket bog in wetter, flatter ground — often forms mosaic
Purple moor-grass and rush pastures occur where drainage is impeded — key competitive threat
Upland acid grassland occurs where heather has been lost to over-grazing or burning — degraded heathland transition
Upland birchwood at lower altitude and in sheltered locations — natural scrub succession if grazing reduced

sources.title: UK BAP Priority Habitat Descriptions — Upland Heathland
sources.specific_claim: BAP priority status, condition indicators, ecosystem functions

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Favourable / Prime
condition_grade_descriptors.description: All positive indicators present. Heather at multiple age stages, bilberry vigorous, lichens and Sphagnum present, Molinia and Nardus <5% cover, no bracken, ground-nesting birds present. UKHab condition: Favourable.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Unfavourable recovering
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Most positive indicators present. Heather recovering but age structure incomplete. Molinia or Nardus 5-15%. Some bracken encroachment beginning. Management intervention in place and effective. Trajectory improving.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Moderate / Unfavourable no change
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Mixed indicators. Heather present but reduced or degraded. Molinia or Nardus 15-25%. Bilberry declining. No active management or management not yet effective. Neither improving nor worsening significantly.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Poor / Unfavourable declining
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Mainly negative indicators. Heather sparse or all degenerate. Molinia or Nardus >25%. Bracken encroaching. Bare peat or poaching visible. Active degradation underway. Urgent management needed.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Transitional / Reclassify
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Heather <10% cover. Dominated by grasses, bracken, or bare peat. No longer functioning as heathland. Reclassify as g1b, g1c, or f2b depending on dominant vegetation. Major restoration intervention required.

grade_field_indicators: Heather cover (%) — assess in representative 1m² quadrats
Heather age structure — walk transect noting dominant growth stage (pioneer/building/mature/degenerate)
Molinia cover (%) — key competitive threat indicator
Nardus cover (%) — indicator of historic overgrazing
Bilberry vigour — present and healthy / declining / absent
Evidence of ground-nesting bird activity — footprints, droppings, calls, nests (observe from distance)

sources.title: SSSI Condition Assessment Criteria — Upland Heathland
sources.specific_claim: Condition assessment criteria and unfavourable/favourable thresholds for upland heathland

forage.overall_palatability_notes: Highly variable by season, heather growth stage, and animal type. Young heather shoots (pioneer phase) are preferred. Mature and degenerate heather is significantly less palatable. Calluna is evergreen and available year-round but is a supplementary rather than primary forage. Score reflects upland context where it is the dominant available forage in winter.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Highly variable by altitude, aspect, management. Pioneer-phase heather yields higher than mature or degenerate. Includes all above-ground herbage accessible to grazing animals. DATA GAP: robust published yield data for upland heathland is limited — these figures are indicative. Sourcing L1-L2 data is a Phase 2A research priority.
Estimated. As Grade 1 but heather age structure less favourable.
Estimated. Heather largely mature or degenerate — lower accessible yield, Molinia and Nardus may contribute.
Estimated. Degraded heathland — mixed grass/heath sward. Very low heather contribution.

forage_yield_literature.notes: DATA FLAG: These figures are derived from general upland agronomy knowledge and limited published data. A dedicated research session is needed to source L1-L2 data from NatureScot, SRUC, or academic literature before these figures should be used in client-facing outputs.

sources.title: Indicative yield estimate — upland heathland
sources.specific_claim: Yield range estimate based on upland consultancy experience — requires L1-L2 verification

seasonal_forage_curve.spring: Heather growth begins from March/April depending on altitude. New shoots most palatable. Other upland forbs and grasses begin growth — important supplement to winter-dried heather.
seasonal_forage_curve.summer: Active heather growth. Flowering July-September — important nectar source, do not graze during flowering if pollinator habitat is a priority. Some grass growth supplement available.
seasonal_forage_curve.autumn: Post-flowering heather still available. Bilberry and other forbs beginning to senesce. Grasses slowing. Animals begin to rely more heavily on heather.
seasonal_forage_curve.winter: Heather evergreen and available throughout. The critical value of heathland in the rotational grazing system — available when grass swards are rested or dormant. Quality low but provides bulk and roughage.

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: Benchmark heathland grazer. Browses heather and woody vegetation. Creates valuable disturbance.
Excellent heathland manager. Lighter than Highland in some lines — less poaching risk.
Lightweight — critical advantage on fragile heath soils. Underused.
Native upland sheep. Selective grazer on heather shoots.
Most common hill sheep. Selects grass over heather — risk of grass bias increasing at high stocking.
CRITICAL: Grazes Molinia in preference to heather — uniquely valuable where Molinia is suppressing heather regeneration. Specified for many upland SSSI management plans.
Primitive breed, near-natural grazing behaviour. Good for rewilding contexts.
Consumes rush and coarse vegetation. Good extensive heathland manager.
Hardy, good heathland and moorland grazer.

unsuitable_animals.reason: Too heavy for fragile heath soils, insufficient hardiness, requires supplementary feeding.
Insufficient hardiness, select improved vegetation preferentially, not suited to extensive heathland.

timing_windows.preferred_season_rationale: Avoids ground-nesting bird season (March-August). Heather browsing in dormant season reduces rank growth without removing critical summer structure. Grazing after seed set allows grass species to reproduce.
timing_windows.avoid: March to August — ground-nesting bird disturbance risk (red grouse, golden plover, curlew, dunlin, merlin). Also avoid wet ground in winter where poaching risk is high.
timing_windows.cattle_timing_note: Cattle most useful for autumn/winter scrub and rank heather management. Even brief summer cattle grazing can cause significant poaching damage to fragile heath soils — requires careful management.
timing_windows.sheep_timing_note: Year-round light sheep grazing traditional on many upland heaths. Heavy winter sheep grazing risks overgrazing heather tips. Remove or reduce stocking if Molinia or Nardus cover increasing.
timing_windows.ponies_timing_note: Year-round if at low density. Ponies create valuable disturbance through pawing and rolling.

management.intensity_guidance: Light to moderate grazing essential. Overgrazing is the primary management threat to upland heathland in the UK. Indicative stocking rates: cattle 0.1-0.3 LU/ha, sheep 0.3-0.8 LU/ha (adjust based on condition grade — lower at Grade 3-4 until recovery underway). Mixed grazing (cattle + sheep or ponies) preferred over single-species — creates more diverse structure. Hebridean sheep particularly valuable at 0.1-0.2 ewes/ha as Molinia control tool. Monitor heather cover and Molinia annually. Rest from grazing for 3-5 years may be needed in severely degraded areas (Grade 4).
management.monitoring_protocol: Annual: walk representative transects (minimum 100m) recording heather cover %, dominant heather growth stage, Molinia/Nardus cover %, bracken extent. Photograph from fixed points at same time each year. Record ground-nesting bird evidence during May survey. Check for poaching/bare peat at cattle access points. 5-yearly: formal NVC quadrat survey to track community composition change. Flag any shift toward g1b composition. Reference condition against UKHab/SSSI condition assessment criteria.

bng_unit_value.value_note: High value BAP priority habitat. In favourable condition (Grade 1) achieves high biodiversity unit values under BNG Metric 4.0. Exact values depend on condition assessment score, strategic significance factor, and local distinctiveness. Do not apply a fixed unit value without site-specific condition assessment.

sources.title: Biodiversity Metric 4.0
sources.specific_claim: Upland heathland is a BAP priority habitat with high unit values — condition assessment required for site-specific calculation

key_positive_indicators: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi (at higher altitude — very good condition indicator)
Rubus chamaemorus (cloudberry — montane, excellent condition indicator)

forage_yield_actual — requires plate meter readings from real sites
species_list — full population requires Phase 2B NVC species work
data_completeness.notes: Habitat identity, ecology, condition indicators, and management plan are well-sourced (L1-L2). Forage yield data is currently estimated and flagged — this is the primary gap. Species list is a skeleton pending Phase 2B. This record is fit for professional reference on the ecological and management dimensions. Do not use yield figures in client-facing outputs until Phase 2A research session is complete.
